title: Psalms 138
subtitle: I Will Praise You with All My Heart

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “You Have Exalted Your Name and Your Word Above All Things”

heb.paragraph: "You have so exalted your solemn decree (dābār) that it surpasses your fame" (v.2). David praises before the "gods" (divine council or idol-temples, v.1). He bows toward the temple (v.2). When David called, God answered with boldness in his soul (v.3). The extraordinary claim: God’s word is exalted above his name. His promises exceed even his reputation.

notes.note: "I will praise you with all my heart; before the gods I will sing your praise." Worship that confronts rival powers. "You have exalted your name and your word above all things."
"When I called, you answered me; you greatly emboldened me." Answered prayer produces boldness. God’s response strengthens David’s soul.

refs.note: Every knee will bow and tongue confess—kings acknowledging the LORD.
The kings of the earth bring their glory into the new Jerusalem.
Nations will come to your light, and kings to the brightness of your dawn.

hist.context: Psalm 138 opens the final Davidic collection (138-145). David boldly declares he will praise YHWH 'before the gods'—whether angelic beings, pagan deities, or earthly rulers. The temple-oriented worship ('toward your holy temple') expresses confidence that even kings will join the praise when they hear God's promises. This is David as royal theologian, anticipating universal recognition.

sections.header: Verses 4–8 — “Though I Walk in the Midst of Trouble, You Preserve My Life”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD will vindicate me; your love, LORD, endures forever — do not abandon the works of your hands (gāmar)" (v.8). Kings will praise when they hear God’s words (v.4). Though God is exalted, he looks kindly on the lowly (v.6). Though David walks in trouble, God preserves his life (v.7). The closing plea: don’t abandon your handiwork. God started something in David; David asks him to finish it.

notes.note: "Though the LORD is exalted, he looks kindly on the lowly; but the proud he knows from afar." The distance paradox: God is near to the humble and far from the proud. Proximity is determined by posture.

"Though I walk in the midst of trouble, you preserve my life. The LORD will fulfil his purpose for me." Trouble does not derail God’s purpose. "Do not abandon the works of your hands" — the plea that God finish what he started.

refs.note: God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
God's power is made perfect in weakness; strength comes in trouble.
God works all things together for good—fulfilling his purpose for his people.

hist.context: David's confidence rests on divine character: the LORD 'preserves the lowly but brings low the proud.' Even in the midst of trouble, God stretches out his hand against enemies. The closing affirmation—'The LORD will fulfill his purpose for me'—expresses covenant assurance. God does not abandon what his hands have made. This psalm models the trust that sustains believers in adversity.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Lord will vindicate me; your love, Lord, endures forever— do not abandon the works of your hands.ESVThe LORD will fulfill his purpose for me; your steadfast love, O LORD, endures forever. Do not forsake the work of your hands.NIVMay all the kings of the earth praise you, Lord, when they hear what you have decreed.ESVAll the kings of the earth shall give you thanks, O LORD, for they have heard the words of your mouth,

rec.text: ""I will praise you with all my heart; before the gods I will sing your praise." Worship that confronts rival powers. "You have exalted your name and your word above all things.""

rows.label: Word Above Name — 138:1–3
rows.text: "You exalted your WORD above all things." Praise before rivals. Called and answered with boldness.

hebtext.note: "You have so exalted your solemn decree (dābār) that it surpasses your fame" (v.2). David praises before the "gods" (divine council or idol-temples, v.1). He bows toward the temple (v.2). When David called, God answered with boldness in his soul (v.3). The extraordinary claim: God’s word is exalted above his name. His promises exceed even his reputation.

"The LORD will vindicate me; your love, LORD, endures forever — do not abandon the works of your hands (gāmar)" (v.8). Kings will praise when they hear God’s words (v.4). Though God is exalted, he looks kindly on the lowly (v.6). Though David walks in trouble, God preserves his life (v.7). The closing plea: don’t abandon your handiwork. God started something in David; David asks him to finish it.

themes.note: Faith: God’s word exalted above his name. Mercy: near to the lowly. Worship: bold praise before rivals.
